user_stories.goal: Generate a clear, day-by-day personalised city itinerary by submitting destination, trip length, budget level, interests, and travel style to the backend API
user_stories.benefit: Quickly get a practical plan I can follow each day of my trip
acceptance_criteria: The Flask API exposes GET /health which returns HTTP 200 when healthy
The Flask API exposes POST /api/itinerary and accepts JSON with destination, days, budget, interests, and travel_style
Valid POST /api/itinerary returns HTTP 200 and a JSON body containing top-level fields: destination, days, budget, interests, travel_style, overview, itinerary, and tips
For valid requests the itinerary array length equals the requested days
Invalid or malformed input returns HTTP 400 with a JSON error object explaining the validation failure

user_stories.goal: Use the web form to submit trip parameters and view a rendered, readable day-by-day itinerary on the page
user_stories.benefit: Easily interact with the planner in the browser without calling the API manually
acceptance_criteria: The frontend form uses stable element IDs: plannerForm, destination, days, budget, interests, travel_style, resultsContainer, daysContainer, formMessage, errorMessage
The frontend implements stable functions: collectFormData, validateFormData, setLoading, showError, renderItinerary, renderDay, renderActivity, and formatActivityText
The frontend calls POST /api/itinerary and handles HTTP 200 responses by rendering the overview, itinerary, and tips into resultsContainer/daysContainer
The frontend never creates an image preview area (image integration handled later)
Frontend rendering must show human-readable activity titles/descriptions and must not render raw '[object Object]' for activity data

user_stories.goal: Rely on a stable response contract so automated tests and downstream components can parse itinerary data deterministically
user_stories.benefit: Automated pytest checks and Docker deployment succeed without flaky behavior
acceptance_criteria: Successful itinerary responses contain the required top-level keys in a deterministic order and format suitable for automated checks
Each itinerary day object includes numeric day, morning, afternoon, evening, and budget_note fields; activity fields may be objects but must render as readable strings in the frontend
Estimated costs are numeric values and are adjusted by the provided budget input (not strings like 'low/medium/high')
The service behavior is deterministic (no random seeds changing between runs) so tests and CI/Docker reproduce the same structure given the same input

user_stories.goal: Ensure multi-day itineraries do not repetitively present the same limited activities and each day has a distinct focus
user_stories.benefit: Users receive varied, realistic daily plans that feel tailored and avoid boring repetition
acceptance_criteria: Each declared interest is expanded into several concrete activities before building the itinerary (e.g., 'food' expands into multiple food-related activities)
Activity selection rotates across morning, afternoon, and evening slots and uses destination, day number, time slot, and user interests to avoid repeating the same generic fallback each day
Each day should attempt a different theme or planning focus when possible (e.g., 'historic', 'foodie', 'outdoors')
Activity titles and descriptions are human-readable strings (no raw dictionaries or '[object Object]')

user_stories.role: Developer responsible for error handling and frontend-backend contract
user_stories.goal: Provide clear validation, stable IDs/functions, and predictable error responses so the frontend can present helpful feedback
user_stories.benefit: Users get immediate, actionable error messages and the UI code remains aligned with the API contract
acceptance_criteria: POST /api/itinerary validates required fields (destination, days, budget, interests, travel_style) and returns HTTP 400 with a JSON error object for missing/invalid values
The frontend validateFormData function mirrors server-side validation rules and displays errors in errorMessage
The frontend uses setLoading and showError to communicate request state and errors consistently using the stable IDs
The frontend renderActivity and formatActivityText functions produce readable text for any activity objects returned by the API
